title: Dog Preputial Discharge: Understanding Yellow or Green Fluid
meta_desc: Noticing yellow or green discharge from your dog's prepuce? Learn what it means, the urgency level, and how photos help your vet with triage.
content_html:
What is Preputial Discharge?

Finding discharge at the tip of your dog's penis or prepuce (the sheath) can be concerning for any pet owner. This fluid is often 'smegma,' a natural collection of skin cells, oils, and moisture. While a small amount of white or pale yellow discharge is normal, a change to a thicker yellow or green consistency usually indicates balanoposthitis. This is a common inflammation or mild infection of the penile sheath that occurs when the natural bacterial balance is disrupted.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for yellow or green preputial discharge is generally Low. In most cases, if your dog is otherwise acting like themselves—eating well, staying hydrated, and urinating without straining—this is not an immediate medical emergency. You should, however, schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian to address the underlying inflammation. If you notice symptoms like lethargy, a loss of appetite, or difficulty urinating, the urgency level may increase.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact your vet or an emergency service, providing a clear photo of the discharge can be incredibly helpful. Because dogs often lick the area clean, the discharge may not be visible by the time you arrive at the clinic. A photo allows the veterinary team to see the exact color, consistency, and volume of the fluid. This visual evidence helps them differentiate between normal physiological discharge and a clinical infection, ensuring your dog receives the most appropriate level of care.

faqs.question: Is yellow discharge always an infection?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. A small amount of light yellow smegma can be normal for male dogs. However, thick, bright yellow or green discharge usually points toward a mild bacterial infection or irritation that requires a veterinary exam.

faqs.question: Can I clean the area at home?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe the area with a soft cloth and warm water. Avoid using harsh soaps, alcohol, or human wet wipes, as these can cause significant irritation to the sensitive mucosal tissue.

faqs.question: When does this become a more urgent situation?
faqs.answer: Seek veterinary care more quickly if you see blood in the discharge, if the area appears very swollen or red, if your dog is constantly licking the area, or if he seems to have pain when trying to urinate.
